scanner_description: Detects framework security features that are available but not enabled — missing helmet, absent rate limits, insecure session config, leaked NEXT_PUBLIC secrets, DEBUG=True in Django, APP_DEBUG in Laravel, dangerouslySetInnerHTML with user data, webhook handlers without signature verification, unsafe file upload configuration.

rules.name: helmet() Not Installed (Express)
rules.note: Express app without helmet — missing security headers: X-Content-Type-Options, X-Frame-Options, HSTS, CSP, and more.
rules.remediation: npm install helmet\napp.use(helmet())

rules.name: No Rate Limiting (Express)
rules.note: API endpoints without rate limiting — vulnerable to brute force and DoS attacks.
rules.remediation: npm install express-rate-limit\napp.use(rateLimit({ windowMs: 15*60*1000, max: 100 }))

rules.name: No Request Size Limit (Express)
rules.note: express.json() without size limit — vulnerable to large payload DoS.
rules.remediation: app.use(express.json({ limit: '10kb' }))

rules.name: Session Cookie Missing Secure Flag (Express)
rules.note: Session cookie without secure flag — transmitted over HTTP, interceptable on public WiFi.
rules.remediation: session({ cookie: { secure: true, httpOnly: true, sameSite: 'strict' } })

rules.name: No Security Headers in next.config (Next.js)
rules.note: Next.js config has no X-Frame-Options header configured. Missing clickjacking protection.
rules.remediation: Add headers() to next.config.js returning security header array. See: https://nextjs.org/docs/advanced-features/security-headers

rules.name: Source Maps Enabled in Production (Next.js)
rules.note: Source maps expose original source code to anyone inspecting the browser. Attackers can read your entire codebase.
rules.remediation: Remove productionBrowserSourceMaps: true from next.config.js

rules.name: Secret in NEXT_PUBLIC_ Variable (Next.js)
rules.note: NEXT_PUBLIC_ vars are bundled into client JavaScript. Any secret here is visible to every user.
rules.remediation: Remove the NEXT_PUBLIC_ prefix. Access server-side only via process.env.SECRET_NAME in API routes.

rules.name: DEBUG = True in Django Settings
rules.note: Django debug mode exposes stack traces, SQL queries, and environment variables to any user on error.
rules.remediation: Set DEBUG = False in production. Use environment variable: DEBUG = os.environ.get('DEBUG', 'False') == 'True'

rules.name: ALLOWED_HOSTS = ['*'] (Django)
rules.note: Wildcard ALLOWED_HOSTS — accepts requests for any hostname. Host header attacks possible.
rules.remediation: Set specific domains: ALLOWED_HOSTS = ['yourdomain.com', 'www.yourdomain.com']

rules.name: Default/Weak SECRET_KEY (Django)
rules.note: Django SECRET_KEY is the default or a weak placeholder. Used for signing sessions, CSRF tokens, and more.
rules.remediation: Generate: python -c "from django.core.management.utils import get_random_secret_key; print(get_random_secret_key())"

rules.name: HTTPS Not Enforced (Django)
rules.note: SECURE_SSL_REDIRECT not enabled or explicitly disabled — HTTP traffic not redirected to HTTPS.
rules.remediation: Add: SECURE_SSL_REDIRECT = True\nSECURE_HSTS_SECONDS = 31536000

rules.name: Debug Mode Enabled (Flask)
rules.note: Flask debug mode exposes the Werkzeug debugger — allows arbitrary code execution from the browser.
rules.remediation: Set debug=False in production. Use FLASK_DEBUG=0 environment variable.

rules.name: Weak SECRET_KEY (Flask)
rules.note: Flask secret key is a weak/default value. Sessions can be forged.
rules.remediation: app.secret_key = os.environ['SECRET_KEY'] # Generate: python -c "import secrets; print(secrets.token_hex(32))"

rules.name: CSRF Protection Partially Disabled (Rails)
rules.note: protect_from_forgery has exceptions — some actions are CSRF-unprotected.
rules.remediation: Remove except: clause or ensure excepted actions don't modify state.

rules.name: force_ssl Disabled (Rails)
rules.note: SSL not enforced — HTTP traffic accepted.
rules.remediation: Set config.force_ssl = true in config/environments/production.rb

rules.name: APP_DEBUG=true (Laravel)
rules.note: Laravel debug mode exposes stack traces, environment variables, and database queries.
rules.remediation: Set APP_DEBUG=false in production .env file.

rules.name: dangerouslySetInnerHTML with User Data (React)
rules.note: User-controlled data passed to dangerouslySetInnerHTML — classic XSS vulnerability.
rules.remediation: Sanitize with DOMPurify: dangerouslySetInnerHTML={{ __html: DOMPurify.sanitize(userInput) }}

rules.name: v-html Directive with Potential User Data (Vue)
rules.note: v-html renders raw HTML — XSS risk if the bound value contains user input.
rules.remediation: Use {{ interpolation }} for text. If HTML is needed, sanitize with DOMPurify first.

rules.name: Stripe Webhook Handler Missing constructEvent
rules.note: A Stripe webhook route is registered but the file never calls stripe.webhooks.constructEvent. Without signature verification, anyone can POST fake events (payment_succeeded, customer.subscription.created) and trigger your fulfillment logic.
rules.remediation: stripe.webhooks.constructEvent(req.body, req.headers['stripe-signature'], process.env.STRIPE_WEBHOOK_SECRET). The body must be a raw Buffer — register express.raw({ type: 'application/json' }) on this route.

rules.name: GitHub Webhook Handler Missing Signature Verification
rules.note: A GitHub webhook route is registered but the file never references X-Hub-Signature-256. GitHub signs every webhook with your secret — without checking it, anyone can spoof pushes, issue events, or PR merges to your handler.
rules.remediation: Read req.headers['x-hub-signature-256'], compute crypto.createHmac('sha256', secret).update(rawBody).digest('hex'), prefix with 'sha256=', and compare with crypto.timingSafeEqual.

rules.name: Clerk Webhook Handler Missing svix Verification
rules.note: A Clerk webhook route is registered but the file never calls Webhook.verify() from svix. Clerk events (user.created, session.created) are signed with your Svix secret — without verification, anyone can forge user lifecycle events.
rules.remediation: Use the svix library: new Webhook(process.env.CLERK_WEBHOOK_SECRET).verify(payload, headers). It throws if the signature is invalid.

rules.name: Polar Webhook Handler Missing Signature Verification
rules.note: A Polar webhook route is registered without calling validateEvent or checking the polar-signature header. Polar manages payments and licenses — a spoofed event can grant a free Pro license or reverse a refund.
rules.remediation: Use Polar's SDK helper validateEvent(payload, headers, webhookSecret), or verify the polar-signature header manually with HMAC-SHA256 + crypto.timingSafeEqual.

rules.name: Linear Webhook Handler Missing Signature Verification
rules.note: A Linear webhook route is registered but the file never reads the linear-signature header. Linear signs every webhook with your endpoint secret — without verification, anyone can POST fake issue.created or comment.created events.
rules.remediation: Read req.headers['linear-signature'], compute crypto.createHmac('sha256', secret).update(rawBody).digest('hex'), compare with crypto.timingSafeEqual.

rules.name: Slack Events Endpoint Missing X-Slack-Signature Verification
rules.note: A Slack events endpoint is registered without verifying X-Slack-Signature. Slack signs every request with v0=HMAC-SHA256(timestamp + body, signing_secret) — without checking it, attackers can fake slash commands, message events, and interactivity payloads.
rules.remediation: Read x-slack-request-timestamp + x-slack-signature, recompute the signature, and compare with crypto.timingSafeEqual. Reject requests where the timestamp is more than 5 minutes old (replay protection).

rules.name: Shopify Webhook Handler Missing HMAC Verification
rules.note: A Shopify webhook route is registered without checking X-Shopify-Hmac-Sha256. Shopify signs every webhook with your shared secret — without verification, anyone can POST fake order.paid or order.cancelled events.
rules.remediation: Read x-shopify-hmac-sha256, compute crypto.createHmac('sha256', secret).update(rawBody).digest('base64'), compare with crypto.timingSafeEqual.

rules.name: Webhook Handler Without Any Signature Check
rules.note: A /webhook/ route is registered and the entire file contains no signature-verification primitives (constructEvent, createHmac, timingSafeEqual, vendor signature headers). Treating an unsigned webhook as authenticated is a common AI mistake — any internet client can fire your handler.
rules.remediation: Identify which vendor sends this webhook and verify their signature header. Generic pattern: read the signature header, compute crypto.createHmac('sha256', secret).update(rawBody).digest('hex'), compare with crypto.timingSafeEqual.

rules.name: HMAC Compared With == Instead of timingSafeEqual
rules.note: An HMAC digest is compared with == / === / !==. String equality returns false on the first mismatched byte, leaking timing information — a remote attacker can recover a valid signature one byte at a time. AI-generated webhook code defaults to == constantly.
rules.remediation: Replace with crypto.timingSafeEqual(Buffer.from(expected, 'hex'), Buffer.from(received, 'hex')). Both Buffers must be the same length — return early on length mismatch.

rules.name: Stripe constructEvent Called With JSON.stringify(req.body)
rules.note: constructEvent receives JSON.stringify(req.body) — Stripe verifies against the EXACT bytes it sent, but JSON.stringify re-serializes with different whitespace and key ordering. Signature verification will fail in production. AI 'fixes' this by passing a parsed object, breaking the security.
rules.remediation: Register express.raw({ type: 'application/json' }) on the webhook route so req.body is a Buffer, then pass it directly: constructEvent(req.body, sig, secret). Never JSON.stringify before constructEvent.

rules.name: Multer Configured Without fileFilter (Express)
rules.note: multer() is called without a fileFilter. Any uploaded file is accepted — including .exe, .php, .html, or .svg with embedded scripts. The MIME type can be set by the client and lies.
rules.remediation: Add a fileFilter that checks file.mimetype AND the buffer's magic bytes against an allowlist: multer({ fileFilter: (req, file, cb) => cb(null, ALLOWED.includes(file.mimetype)) }).

rules.name: Multer Configured Without limits (Express)
rules.note: multer() is called without a limits config. Uploads can be arbitrarily large — a single client can exhaust disk or memory and DoS the server.
rules.remediation: Set explicit per-upload limits: multer({ limits: { fileSize: 5 * 1024 * 1024, files: 5 } }) (5 MB, max 5 files per request).

rules.name: Multer dest Set to Web-Served Directory
rules.note: multer's dest is inside a web-served directory (public/, static/, www/, dist/, build/). Uploaded files are reachable via direct URL — including any .html/.svg/.js the attacker uploads, making this a stored-XSS vector at minimum.
rules.remediation: Store uploads outside the public webroot (e.g., /var/uploads/) and serve them through an authenticated route that sets Content-Disposition: attachment.

rules.name: Formidable Configured Without maxFileSize
rules.note: formidable parses multipart uploads with no maxFileSize cap. The default accepts up to 200 MB per file — a flood of large uploads can fill disk and trip the server.
rules.remediation: Set maxFileSize explicitly: new formidable.IncomingForm({ maxFileSize: 5 * 1024 * 1024 }) (5 MB). Add maxFiles and maxFields too.

rules.name: User-Supplied Filename Used in Path Construction
rules.note: file.originalname is passed directly into path.join / fs.writeFile / fs.createWriteStream / fs.rename. A malicious client can set originalname to ../../etc/passwd or C:\\Windows\\System32\\... and write outside the upload directory. This is path traversal.
rules.remediation: Sanitize with path.basename(file.originalname) before joining, or replace the filename entirely with crypto.randomUUID() + a server-controlled extension.

rules.name: File Upload Extension Allowlist Includes Dangerous Type
rules.note: Upload allowlist contains .svg, .html, .htm, .xml, .js, or .mjs. SVG can carry <script> tags; HTML/JS are obviously executable; XML enables XXE — any of these served from your origin is stored XSS or worse.
rules.remediation: Remove these extensions from the allowlist. If you must accept SVG, sanitize with DOMPurify on upload AND serve with Content-Type: image/svg+xml + a sandbox CSP.

rules.name: Multer diskStorage filename Returns originalname Unsanitized
rules.note: multer.diskStorage's filename callback returns file.originalname. The client controls this string — they can submit ../../shell.php or filename.exe to overwrite arbitrary paths or upload an executable. AI 'preserves' the original name without sanitizing.
rules.remediation: Generate the saved filename server-side: cb(null, crypto.randomUUID() + path.extname(file.originalname)). Only the extension is taken from the client, and only after a strict allowlist check.

rules.name: Busboy Configured Without limits
rules.note: busboy parses multipart uploads without a limits config. Defaults allow unlimited file sizes, field counts, and parts — a single attacker can exhaust memory or fill disk with one request.
rules.remediation: Set explicit limits: Busboy({ headers: req.headers, limits: { fileSize: 5*1024*1024, files: 5, fields: 20 } }).
